coredata.dc:title: A graphene quantum dots-Pb2+ based fluorescent switch for selective and sensitive determination of D-penicillamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qi
Li, Lingfang
Wu, Tingxuan
Kong, Xiangpeng
Ma, Qingguo
Ma, Chunlei
coredata.dc:description: Taking consideration of metal-induced fluorescence quenching and excellent coordination effect of D-penicillamine (D-PA), a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based fluorescent switch for D-PA detection was designed and established firstly with the help of lead ions. GQDs obtained from citric acids made them rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, giving GQDs the ability to combine with lead ions. As anticipated, the fluorescence intensity was quenched by Pb2+ through electron transfer process. Further, the addition of D-PA effectively recovered the fluorescence due to the departure of Pb2+ from GQDs aroused by the strong coordination between D-PA and Pb2+. Thus, a fluorescent switch was activated for D-PA detection. The fluorescence recovery efficiencies were found to be proportional to the concentration of D-PA in the range of 0.6–50 μmol L− 1 and the detection limit was 0.47 μmol L− 1. The real sample detection was performed in human urea sample and satisfactory recoveries of 96.84%–102.13% were obtained. The GQDs-Pb2+ based fluorescent switch sensing method was firstly established with low detection limit and wide linear range, making it a supplement and improvement for D-PA detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent switch
Lead ions
D-penicillamine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Pb2+ and D-PA to GQDs
D-PA detection in human urine sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi
Discipline Leaders of Taiyuan Institute of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (STIP, No. 2019L0918 ) and the Program for the (Reserved) Discipline Leaders of Taiyuan Institute of Technology . We thank the Analysis and Testing Center of Taiyuan Institute of Technology for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of as-prepared GQDs, the scale bars were 20 nm and 2 nm, respectively.
(A) The size distribution of as-prepared GQDs. The XPS (B) full scan spectrum, (C) C 1s spectrum and (D) O 1s spectrum of the resultant GQDs.

(A) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (red) and fluorescence emission spectrum (blue) of GQDs. Inset: photographs of GQDs under (1) sunlight and (2) UV (365 nm, center) illumination. (B) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs (black), GQDs+Pb2+ (red) and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA (blue), respectively. Inset: photographs of (3) GQDs, (4) GQDs+Pb2+ and (5) GQDs+Pb2++D-PA under UV (365 nm, center) illumination. (C) Schematic illustration of GQDs based fluorescent switch for D-PA determination.
The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra at 460 nm from GQDs (black), GQDs+Pb2+ (red) and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA (blue), respectively.
(A) The fluorescence intensity of GQDs, GQDs+Pb2+ and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA solutions under different pH; (B) The fluorescence recovery efficiency (ΔF/F0) of the switch under different pH of PBS buffers.

(A) The linear calibration plot for D-PA detection using GQDs-Pb2+ fluorescent switch. (B) The selectivity of GQDs-Pb2+ fluorescent switch for D-PA in the presence of Na+, Zn2+, Fe3+, NH4 +, ascorbic acid, thiourea, uric acid, glucose, dopamine and urea.
Parameters of multi-exponential fits to the fluorescence decay.
Comparison of the present method with other fluorescent switches for D-PA detection.
Determination of D-PA in human urine sample.

head.ce:title: A graphene quantum dots-Pb2+ based fluorescent switch for selective and sensitive determination of D-penicillamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030008, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030008, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Taking consideration of metal-induced fluorescence quenching and excellent coordination effect of D-penicillamine (D-PA), a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based fluorescent switch for D-PA detection was designed and established firstly with the help of lead ions. GQDs obtained from citric acids made them rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, giving GQDs the ability to combine with lead ions. As anticipated, the fluorescence intensity was quenched by Pb2+ through electron transfer process. Further, the addition of D-PA effectively recovered the fluorescence due to the departure of Pb2+ from GQDs aroused by the strong coordination between D-PA and Pb2+. Thus, a fluorescent switch was activated for D-PA detection. The fluorescence recovery efficiencies were found to be proportional to the concentration of D-PA in the range of 0.6–50 μmol L− 1 and the detection limit was 0.47 μmol L− 1. The real sample detection was performed in human urea sample and satisfactory recoveries of 96.84%–102.13% were obtained. The GQDs-Pb2+ based fluorescent switch sensing method was firstly established with low detection limit and wide linear range, making it a supplement and improvement for D-PA detection.

Schematic illustration of GQDs based fluorescent switch for D-PA determination.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), as a kind of emerging pollutants, have aroused the attention from researchers and the public considering their threats to the human health and ecological environment safety [1–3]. PPCPs represent a class of chemical products used in daily life, including pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and personal hygiene products. Up to now, the global consumption of PPCPs has exceeded 10,000 tons per year and is still growing [4]. What followed is that the active ingredients in these products, such as drugs, antibiotics, preservatives, etc., have leaked into the environment through urine, excreta, wastewater, sewage, fertilizer and so on [5]. Among them, pharmaceuticals can exist in the environment for a long time without being naturally degraded, and can be easily enriched through the food chain, which has seriously damaged the ecological balance and threatened human life safety. Therefore, it is of great significance for sensitive and selective detection of pharmaceuticals.

Penicillamine (2-amino-3-mercapto-3-methylbutanic acid) is widely recognized as the main hydrolytic metabolite of penicillin [6,7]. D-type enantiomer of penicillamine (D-PA) plays an important role in clinical and biomedical areas as an effective therapeutic drug for treatment of several diseases [8,9]. However, improperly intake of D-PA may cause some side effects including oral ulcer, ageustia, erythra and so on [10]. Therefore, the accurate detection of D-PA would attract much interest. Up to date, various kinds of methods have been established for selective detection of D-PA such as high performance liquid chromatography [11,12], capillary electrophoresis [13], flow injection analysis [14], electrochemical sensors [15–18], chemiluminescence assay [19–21], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [22–26] and fluorescence spectrophotometry. Benefiting from their fast, facile and sensitive advantages, fluorimetry with different sensing principles are widely concerned for pharmaceutical analysis. Fluorescent nanomaterials including Au NCs [27,28], Cu NCs [29], MOFs [30], organic nanoparticles [31], CdS quantum dots [32], and carbon nanodots [33,34] have been applied for D-PA determination. Among these methods, fluorescent switches attract much attention owing to their merits of low signal-to-noise ratio and high sensitivity stemming from their special sensing modes. The fluorescence intensities are firstly quenched by heavy metal ions like Fe3+ [35], Cu2+ [36], Hg2+ [37], Au3+ [38] and then the addition of D-PA induces the recovery of fluorescence taking advantage of the favorable coordination capacity of D-PA with metal ions [39,40]. Thus, the fluorescence switches for D-PA determination are achieved. However, there were no lead ions based fluorescent switch reported for D-PA.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely recognized as nanometer-sized fragments of graphene that possess favorable luminescent property [41]. They combine the advantages of both graphene and carbon dots [42], displaying merits in various research areas especially fluorescence. GQDs show excellent fluorescence properties stemming from quantum confinement and edge effects [43], and as a consequence, have been listed as a promising candidate for fluorescent analysis applications. Analytical targets including metal ions [44,45], anions [46,47], small molecules [48–51] have been successfully determined by GQDs.

Herein, a GQDs based fluorescent switch for D-PA determination was designed firstly with the help of lead ions. GQDs were synthesized from citric acids making the nanomaterial surface rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Pb2+ was combined at the edge of GQDs and subsequently resulted in the fluorescence quench. Furthermore, the addition of D-PA triggered the coordination between D-PA and Pb2+, which interdicted the combination of Pb2+ with GQDs and as a consequence restored the fluorescence. Therefore, the fluorescent switch was achieved under the mediation of D-PA and fluorescent determination of D-PA was established. The GQDs-Pb2+ system was firstly applied for D-PA fluorescent sensing and this method improved the fluorometry for D-PA.

Deionized water used throughout the experiment with conductivity of 18.2 MΩ cm− 1 came from a water purification system (ULUPURE, Chengdu, China). D-Penicillamine (D-PA, 98%) and citric acid (AR, 99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Other reagents used in the experiment were at least of analytical grade and were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solutions (0.02 mol L− 1) with different pH values were prepared by titrating a mixed solution (39 mL NaH2PO4 solution and 61 mL Na2HPO4 solution, whose concentrations were both 0.02 mol L− 1) using sodium hydroxide or hydrochloric acid solution to the required pH values. Human urine samples were collected from healthy adult volunteers and diluted for 20 folds before analysis.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of GQDs was performed on a JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) for the morphology study. The size distribution of GQDs was examined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) test on a Nano-ZS90 laser particle sizer (Malvern, UK). The composition and chemical states of the as-prepared GQDs was investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique on a XSAM 800 electron spectrometer (Kratos) using monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs was recorded on a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Puxi, China). The fluorescence spectra and fluorescent intensities of GQDs were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes of products were examined on an FLS 1000 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

GQDs used in this work was synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acids referring to a reported work with some modifications [52]. In a typical process, 2 g citric acid powders were placed into a 50 mL beaker and was heated in a heating mantle at 200 °C. After the solid powders were melted, heating was continued for another 30 min. The solution turned into orange gradually, indicating the formation of GQDs. Then NaOH solution with concentration of 10 mg mL− 1 were added drop by drop under ultrasonication until the product was completely dissolved. At last, the solutions were dialyzed to deionized water for 2 days for purification and stored at 4 °C for future characterization and use. The GQDs powders were obtained through rotary evaporation.

The determination of D-PA by the fluorescent switch of GQDs-Pb2+ system was performed in PBS solution (0.02 mol L− 1) with pH = 7.0 at room temperature. In a typical run, the resultant GQDs solutions were diluted for 10 folds firstly and 100 μL of diluent were added into 700 μL of above-mentioned PBS solutions. Then 100 μL of Pb2+ solutions with concentration of 1 mM were added and mixed evenly. Next, a series of D-PA solutions with different concentrations or deionized water (for control) were added in the GQDs-Pb2+ mixtures. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded immediately at room temperature. All the fluorescence measurements were performed under the identical conditions: the excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm, and the slot widths of the excitation and emission were both 5 nm. The sensitivity and selectivity tests were conducted in triplicate.

For human urine sample detection, 100 μL of sample solution were added into PBS solution (0.02 mol L− 1, pH = 7.0) containing GQDs solution (100 μL), Pb2+ solutions (100 μM) and standard D-PA solution (10.00, 30.00, 50.00 μM) and the final volume was kept at 1 mL. The fluorescence intensity was measured at the above-mentioned conditions and the content of D-PA in human urine sample and recovery were calculated accordingly.

Fig. 1 displayed the TEM and HRTEM images of as-prepared GQDs, respectively. The homo-dispersive nanoparticles were observed in Fig. 1A and a distinct lattice fringe was shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1B). The interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm represented the (110) plane of graphite that was well consistent with the previous report [53]. To acquire the size distribution of this product, dynamic light scattering (DLS) test was performed in Fig. 2A. The results proved that the nanoparticles were ranging from 3.6 to 10.1 nm and an average size of 5.6 nm was obtained. The XPS technique was carried out for testing the chemical composition and state of resultant GQDs. Fig. 2B showed the XPS full scan spectrum of GQDs, from which element C 1s and O 1s were detected. In the partial spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2C), three component peaks at 284.8, 286.2 and 287.5 eV were described to combination form of CC, CO and CO, respectively. While in the partial spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2D), the peak can be split into two peaks at 531.7 and 532.7 eV that were corresponded to the combination form of CO and COH/COC, respectively. Therefore, the above morphology and composition data demonstrated the successful synthesis of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Pb2+ and D-PA to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant GQDs exhibited favorable optical performances. Fig. 3A recorded the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs. As indicated, GQDs showed a broad absorption band ranging from 220 to 400 nm (red line). Further, GQDs displayed a fluorescence emission peak centered at 460 nm (blue line) under the excitation of 360 nm, which emitted bright blue fluorescence (Inset photos in Fig. 3A). Taking consideration of metal-induced fluorescence quenching and excellent coordination effect of D-PA, GQDs were applied for a D-PA mediated fluorescent switch. Fig. 3B recorded the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, GQDs+Pb2+ and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA, respectively. It was demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was dramatically quenched in the presence of Pb2+ (red line) to a turn-off state. Furthermore, the addition of D-PA, however, caused an obvious recovery of the fluorescence intensity (blue line) to a turn-on state. Thus, a GQDs based fluorescent switch was activated by D-PA. To intuitively illustrate this phenomenon, the photos of GQDs, GQDs+Pb2+ and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA were captured under the UV light illumination at 365 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. 3B), in which the vivid variations of blue fluorescence were observed.

Fig. 3C graphically illustrated the D-PA mediated fluorescent switch, where blue shadows stood for the fluorescence of GQDs. Pb2+ was combined on the edge of GQDs and subsequently weakened the fluorescence. Subsequently, Pb2+ was departed from GQDs and combined with D-PA instead and as a consequence the fluorescence was improved upon addition of D-PA. To investigate the fluorescent sensing mechanism of Pb2+ and D-PA, the fluorescence lifetimes were examined in Fig. 4 and Table 1 . The fluorescence lifetimes of GQDs, GQDs+Pb2+ and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA were calculated to be 3.57, 3.51 and 3.54 ns, respectively. Since the fluorescence lifetime almost unchanged during the turn-off and turn-on processes, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is considered to be static quenching effect [54]. Pb2+ was combined at the edge of GQDs through the strong coordination ability between Pb2+ and the carboxyl groups in GQDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching via electron transfer (ET) process [55,56]. As to fluorescence recovery, D-PA played an activate role through competitive combination with Pb2+. It was reported that D-PA and Pb2+ can form a stable complex at a mole ratio of 2 to 1 [57]. Hence, Pb2+ was stripped off the surface of GQDs once D-PA was introduced and consequently the fluorescence was restored.

Owing to the advantageous response of GQDs to Pb2+ and D-PA, the fluorescent switch was concerned as a fluorescent sensor for D-PA detection. As the fluorescence was responded rapidly at room temperature, the pH values of solutions were the key factor to influence the fluorescence sensing. Fig. 5A examined the fluorescence intensities of GQDs, GQDs+Pb2+ and GQDs+Pb2++D-PA under buffer solutions with different pH values and the corresponding fluorescence recovery efficiencies (ΔF/F0) were calculated in Fig. 5B. The results show a pH-dependent fluorescence recovery efficiency that ΔF/F0 improved gradually along with the increase of pH values. Previous studies had proved that D-PA favored to form stable complex with Pb2+ under alkaline condition [57], and this took responsibility for the pH-dependent behavior of fluorescence recovery efficiency.

Under the optimal condition for D-PA determination using the fluorescent switch of GQDs-Pb2+ system, the linear responses were measured in Fig. 6A. It was indicated that the fluorescence recovery efficiencies of the GQDs-Pb2+ system were proportional to the concentration of D-PA in the range of 0.6–50 μmol L− 1, and followed the equation ΔF/F0 = 0.0307 + 0.00467C (R2 = 0.9931), where C was the concentration of D-PA (μmol L− 1), ΔF was the difference of fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-Pb2+ system before and after addition of GSH, and F0 was the original fluorescence intensity of the GQDs. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.47 μmol L− 1 (3σ). Table 2 compared the limit of detection (LOD) and linear range of this method with other reported fluorescent switch methods, in which the present GQDs-Pb2+ showed a satisfactory linear range with low LOD.

Further, the selectivity of this fluorescent switch for D-PA detection against possible coexistences in real samples were investigated. Fig. 6B examined the fluorescence recovery efficiencies of the GQDs-Pb2+ system in the presence of D-PA and some metal ions (Na+, Zn2+, Fe3+) and NH4 +, ascorbic acid, thiourea, uric acid, glucose, dopamine, urea with equal concentrations, respectively. The results demonstrated that except for D-PA, which showed a significant fluorescence recovery, no other substances interfered. Therefore, the GQDs based fluorescent switch showed a good selectivity for D-PA determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: D-PA detection in human urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: The above results indicated that the GQDs based fluorescent switch method possessed the ability to test practical samples and the D-PA content in human urine sample was detected according. The urine sample was diluted for 20 folds before analysis. The results were listed in Table 3 , from which the standard recovery rates of this method were found between 96.84 and 102.13%. Therefore, the fluorescent switch was verified as a reliable method for practically detection of D-PA in real samples.

In general, a GQDs based fluorescent switch was designed and established for sensitive and selective detection of D-PA in this work. GQDs were synthesized from citric acids and it made them rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl, which endow the ability to bind to lead ions. Therefore, the fluorescence was quenched by Pb2+ through electron transfer process. Further, the addition of D-PA, however, induced the competitive combination owing to the strong coordination between D-PA and Pb2+. As a consequence, the fluorescence was recovered and the fluorescent switch for D-PA detection was achieved. A satisfactory linear range covering three orders of magnitude with low LOD was obtained and real sample analysis was performed. The fluorescent switch was firstly constructed using Pb2+, which supplemented and improved the fluorescent methods for D-PA detection.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Qi Wang:Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Lingfang Li:Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology.Tingxuan Wu:Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology.Xiangpeng Kong:Data curation, Methodology.Qingguo Ma:Conceptualization, Methodology.Chunlei Ma:Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (STIP, No.2019L0918) and the Program for the (Reserved) Discipline Leaders of Taiyuan Institute of Technology. We thank the Analysis and Testing Center of Taiyuan Institute of Technology for the material characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water analysis: emerging contaminants and current issues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. D. Richardson, T. A. Ternes, Water analysis: emerging contaminants and current issues, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 398-428.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual purpose microalgae-based biorefinery for treating pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) residues and biodiesel production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Xie, S. -H. Ho, J. Peng, X. J. Xu, C. Chen, Z. F. Zhang, D. -J. Lee, N. Q. Ren, Dual purpose microalgae-based biorefinery for treating pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) residues and biodiesel production, Sci. Total Environ. 668 (2019) 253-261.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Z. Yi, N. H. Tran, T. R. Yin, Y. L. He, K. Y. -H. Gin, Removal of selected PPCPs, EDCs, and antibiotic resistance genes in landfill leachate by a full-scale constructed wetlands system, Water Res. 121 (2017), 46-60.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence, fate and transformation of emerging contaminants in water: an overarching review of the field

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wilkinson, P. S. Hooda, J. Barker, Occurrence, fate and transformation of emerging contaminants in water: An overarching review of the field, Environ. Pollut.231 (2017) 954-970.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical methodologies for the determination of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in sewage sludge: a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Pérez-Lemus, R. López-Serna, S. I. Pérez-Elvira, E. Barrado, Analytical methodologies for the determination of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in sewage sludge: A critical review, Anal. Chim. Acta 1083 (2019) 19-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The story of penicillamine: a difficult birth

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. M. Walshe, The story of penicillamine: A difficult birth, Mov. Disord. 18 (2003) 853-859.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inhibition of carboxypeptidase a by D-penicillamine: mechanism and implications for drug design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. R. Chong, D. S. Auld, Inhibition of carboxypeptidase A by D-penicillamine: mechanism and implications for drug design, Biochemistry 39 (2000) 7580-7588.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel HPLC-electrochemical detection approach for the determination of D-penicillamine in skin specimens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. A. Saracino, C. Cannistraci, F. Bugamelli, E. Morganti, A novel HPLC-electrochemical detection approach for the determination of D-penicillamine in skin specimens, Talanta 103 (2013) 355-360.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of chelating agents on the excretion of copper, zinc and iron in the bile and urine of sheep

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. R. Gooneratne, D. A. Christensen, Effect of chelating agents on the excretion of copper, zinc and iron in the bile and urine of sheep, Vet. J. 153 (1997) 171-178.
W. F. Kean, H. E. Howard-Lock, C. J. L. Lock, Chirality in antirheumatic drugs, Lancet 338 (1991) 1565-1568.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of penicillamine in blood and urine by high performance liquid chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Saetre R., Rabenstein D.L., Determination of penicillamine in blood and urine by high performance liquid chromatography, Anal. Chem. 50 (1978) 276-280.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of tiopronin and D-penicillamine in human urine by liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kusmierek, E. Bald, Simultaneous determination of tiopronin and D-penicillamine in human urine by liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection, Anal. Chim. Acta 590 (2007) 132-137.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of penicillamine in pharmaceuticals and human plasma by capillary electrophoresis with in-column fiber optics light-emitting diode induced fluorescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, H. Yuan, C. Wang, X. Su, L. Hu, D. Xiao, Determination of penicillamine in pharmaceuticals and human plasma by capillary electrophoresis with in-column fiber optics light-emitting diode induced fluorescence detection, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal. 45 (2007) 362-366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective flow-injection biamperometric determination of sulfur-containing amino acids and structurally related compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Catalá Icardo, O. Armenta Estrela, M. Sajewicz, J. V. García Mateo, J. Martínez, Selective flow-injection biamperometric determination of sulfur-containing amino acids and structurally related compounds, Anal. Chim. Acta 438 (2001) 281-289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel strategy for selective determination of d-penicillamine based on molecularly imprinted polypyrrole electrode via the electrochemical oxidation with ferrocyanide

Mazloum-Ardakani, Construction of a nanocomposite sensor by the modification of a carbon-paste electrode with reduced graphene oxide and a hydroquinone derivative: simultaneous determination of glutathione and penicillamine, Anal. Methods 7 (2015) 5538-5544.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple method for determination of D-penicillamine on the carbon paste electrode using cupric ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ghaffarinejad, F. Hashemi, Z. Nodehi, R. Salahandish, A simple method for determination of D-penicillamine on the carbon paste electrode using cupric ions, Bioelectrochemistry 99 (2014) 53-56.
